title: Anxiety in Women: the Under-Discussed Shape
subtitle: On the specific ways anxiety presents in women, and why it goes unrecognised
excerpt: A reading on the presentation of anxiety in women, particularly the hypervigilance-as-normal pattern and the physical symptoms that don't get connected to anxiety. On why women's anxiety is under-diagnosed, undertreated, and often misread as personality traits.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education, drawing on research on gendered presentations of anxiety including Tracy Dennis-Tiwary's Future Tense: Why Anxiety is Good for You (Even Though It Feels Bad) (2022) and the gender gap in anxiety diagnosis literature.

content.text: Anxiety is the most common mental health condition worldwide. It is significantly more common in women than men. Women's anxiety often goes unrecognised: by healthcare providers, by the women themselves, and by the people around them. Part of the reason is that anxiety in women often does not match the cultural picture of it.

The cultural image of anxiety is acute: panic attacks, phobias, visible distress. This form exists but is not the most common one. In women, anxiety more often looks chronic and low-level. Persistent worry that moves from one concern to the next. Trouble settling or resting. Sensitivity to conflict or criticism. A body that stays mildly alert even in safe places. And the constant tracking of what needs doing, what might go wrong, what others need.

Many women with this form of anxiety have never named it. It gets described as personality: being a worrier, a planner, or someone who cares a lot. These descriptions are not wrong. They miss the physical tension underneath.

Several things compound the under-recognition. Women are raised to manage and hide distress rather than show it in ways that prompt help. The alertness and people-watching that come with anxious processing are often rewarded in women. The behaviour gets described as caring, responsible, and socially aware. The anxiety underneath goes unremarked.

Anxiety also has physical symptoms: chest tightness, digestive upset, muscle tension, fatigue, and disturbed sleep. These are often investigated medically and dismissed when no physical cause is found. The anxiety is rarely named as the likely source.

Anxiety is a nervous system state. The amygdala, the brain's threat-detection system, has registered something as requiring alertness, and the body has responded accordingly. This can happen without a consciously identifiable threat. It can happen as a low background hum rather than an acute spike. And it can become so habitual that the body maintains a mild alert state as its default.

Understanding anxiety as a physiological state rather than a character trait changes the approach to working with it. Character is hard to change. Nervous system states are workable: through breath, through somatic practices, through the gradual accumulation of experiences of safety.

Naming anxiety as a nervous system pattern rather than a character flaw tends to reduce the shame attached to it. The shame about being anxious is often more disabling than the anxiety itself. Women who name their chronic worry as anxiety rather than weakness tend to find more self-compassion and practical support.
Written for homemade.education. It draws on research into gender and anxiety and the somatic approach to anxiety care.
